does: This paper investigates whether language models can improve through self-training on their own generated text without external guidance, finding that improvement depends critically on compatibility between the data source and student model rather than inherent data quality. The authors study minimal prompt-free unconditional self-training where base models are fine-tuned on text generated from the BOS token alone. They demonstrate three key empirical findings: synthetic data utility is fundamentally relational rather than intrinsic, with self-generated data proving most effective and same-lineage model transfers outperforming stronger differently-trained sources; common intrinsic metrics like semantic similarity and per-token likelihood fail to predict data utility; and cross-family model transfer substantially underperforms same-family transfer, suggesting the latent capability resurfacing hypothesis where weak self-training amplifies pre-existing model capabilities only under specific compatibility conditions.
connects: This work extends the self-training literature that has long explored learning from unlabeled or model-generated data, building on recent interest in whether models can improve by learning from their own generations. It responds to and critiques prior assumptions about data quality metrics by showing that standard proxies for synthetic data utility—semantic similarity and likelihood—do not transfer across model pairs. The paper engages with foundational questions about what makes training data useful, challenging intrinsic notions of data quality established in curriculum learning and data selection literature. It also connects to broader work on model lineage, transfer learning, and the surprising finding that stronger models do not necessarily provide better training signals, resonating with recent observations about scaling and generalization dynamics in language models.
enables: These findings open several research directions regarding model-data compatibility and synthetic data generation at scale. The relational nature of data utility suggests developing new metrics that capture source-student compatibility rather than absolute data quality, potentially enabling better selection strategies for synthetic data in self-training pipelines. The work points toward understanding what structural or parametric properties determine latent capability compatibility, which could inform more efficient knowledge distillation and transfer learning approaches. Future work might explore whether compatibility can be predicted or optimized before expensive fine-tuning, and whether similar relational properties govern learning from synthetic data in other domains like vision or multimodal learning, with implications for scaling laws and data-efficient training regimes.
